prompt: In the expand–contract (parallel change) pattern, which steps are safe and reversible, and which is the dangerous one to perform last? (Select all statements that are TRUE.)
choices: Adding the new shape alongside the old ('expand') is safe and reversible
Migrating readers and writers to the new shape is done gradually while both shapes work
Removing the old shape ('contract') is the irreversible step and should come last
Removing the old shape first, before anything uses the new shape, is the safest order

prompt: During a migration, new code begins writing data in a format the old code cannot read. Why is this dangerous?
choices: It makes the new system run slower than the old one
It destroys the ability to roll back, because reverting to the old code would break on the new data
It violates the strangler-fig pattern's naming convention
It is harmless as long as the new code is well tested

prompt: A dual-write occasionally succeeds on the new store but fails on the old store, leaving the two inconsistent. What is the appropriate way to handle this?
choices: Assume both writes always succeed and take no further action
Stop dual-writing immediately and delete the old store
Add reconciliation that periodically compares the stores, logs divergences, and converges them
Switch all reads to the new store right away to hide the inconsistency
